statement.markdown: Given the head of a singly linked list, implement a function to traverse the linked list and return the number of nodes in the linked list. The function should take in the head of the linked list and return an integer representing the number of nodes.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a singly linked list represented as a node with value and next pointer. The next pointer is null for the last node.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be an integer representing the number of nodes in the linked list.
statement.notes: You can assume that the linked list does not contain any cycles.

hints.text: Start with a simple iterative solution to traverse the linked list.

editorial.title: LinkedList Traversal Function Editorial
editorial.intuition: To solve this problem, we can start at the head of the linked list and iterate through each node, updating a counter variable with the number of nodes visited.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a recursive approach to traverse the linked list, but this will likely be slower and less efficient than an iterative solution.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is an iterative approach that uses a simple counter variable to keep track of the number of nodes visited.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness is straightforward: we start at the head of the linked list and keep track of the number of nodes visited until we reach the end of the linked list.
editorial.dryRun: Here's an example of how the function would work with a linked list of nodes [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].

starterCode.javascript: function solve(head) { // TODO: implement ; return null; }

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: int solve(ListNode* head) { int count = 0; while (head) { count++; head = head->next->next; } return count; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public int solve(ListNode head) { int count = 0; while (head) { count++; head = head.next.next; } return count; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def solve(self, head: ListNode) -> int: count = 0; while head: count += 1; head = head.next.next; return count;
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function solve(head) { let count = 0; while (head) { count++; head = head.next.next; } return count; }

solutions.python:
class Solution: def solve(self, head: ListNode) -> int: count = 0; while head: count += 1; head = head.next; return count;
if __name__ == '__main__':
    head = ListNode(1)
    head.next = ListNode(2)
    head.next.next = ListNode(3)
    head.next.next.next = ListNode(4)
    head.next.next.next.next = ListNode(5)
    print(Solution().solve(head))
